The coastal to inner-shelf muddy belt and offshore tidal sand ridges of the East China Sea (ECS) constitute a coupled sedimentary pattern during the postglacial deposition, challenging the prevailing paradigm of seaward, unidirectional dispersal of sedimentary organic carbon (OC) in coastal to shelf regions worldwide. Here we investigate characteristics and temporal heterogeneity of diverse sedimentary OC in specific depositional environments during the past 14 kyr based on dual carbon isotopes (13C and 14C) and multi-biomarkers in sediment core ECS-1302 of the muddy belt. Sedimentary OC-14C ages deviated anomalously from their depositional ages during the postglacial transgression, alongside a decreasing proportion of terrestrial OC, supporting energetic forcing reintroduced pre-aged OC from offshore into coastal to inner-shelf areas, associating with shoreward, fine-grained sediment reworking of the paleo-Yangtze River estuary. This study underscores dual-directional cycles of sedimentary OC should be fully considered in exploring carbon cycle for large river- and tide-dominated marginal seas. Sedimentary organic carbon ages deviated from depositional ages, with falling terrestrial organic carbon fraction, suggesting energetic forcing reintroduced pre-aged organic carbon from offshore into coastal to inner-shelf areas, based on East China Sea coastal muddy sediment core analysis.

item.title: Magnetotelluric Imaging of Slab Subduction and Rollback: Constraints on Mantle‐Driven Orogenic Evolution in the Jiangnan Orogen
item.dc:creator: Yuexin You
Jiayong Yan
Juzhi Deng
Kun Zhang
Hui Yu
Hui Chen
Yufeng Chen
Xu Wang
Min Feng
Yidan Wen
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract The Neoproterozoic Jiangnan Orogen (JNO) in South China, a fundamental suture amalgamating the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks, provides key insights into orogenic processes. However, poorly constrained lithospheric architecture in the central JNO sustains debates regarding the genesis of Neoproterozoic magmatism. Here, we present a high‐resolution, 3‐D resistivity model extending to 135 km depth, derived from broadband magnetotelluric data across the central JNO. The model reveals a predominantly resistive crust, disrupted by prominent conductors within the middle‐to‐lower crust beneath the Jiuling batholith. In the lithospheric mantle, a sharp electrical discontinuity coincides with the Early Neoproterozoic collisional zone (ca. 850–820 Ma), separating a resistive northwest from a conductive southeast. Integrated with geophysical and thermodynamic modeling, our findings suggest that present‐day partial melting beneath the Jiuling batholith is unlikely. Instead, the crustal conductors are interpreted as fossilized remnants of ancient tectono‐magmatic processes, likely representing trapped saline fluids or conductive mineral networks (e.g., graphite or sulfides). The upper mantle conductor is similarly interpreted as a fossilized subduction‐modified domain, potentially enriched in fluorine‐bearing phlogopite. Geodynamically, we propose that rollback and subsequent break‐off of the Paleo–South China Ocean slab induced substantial asthenospheric upwelling. This thermal perturbation drove extensive remelting of the thickened accretionary crust, generating the voluminous Jiuling granites. Our results delineate an integrated evolutionary pathway from oceanic subduction to mantle‐driven orogenesis, providing new insights into crustal maturation and Phanerozoic reactivation in ancient accretionary systems. Published: 2026-05-23T07:26:16+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.564 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuexin You; Jiayong Yan; Juzhi Deng; Kun Zhang; Hui Yu; Hui Chen; Yufeng Chen; Xu Wang; Min Feng; Yidan Wen

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2025jb033631

Relevance: 0.564 (consider)

Abstract The Neoproterozoic Jiangnan Orogen (JNO) in South China, a fundamental suture amalgamating the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks, provides key insights into orogenic processes. However, poorly constrained lithospheric architecture in the central JNO sustains debates regarding the genesis of Neoproterozoic magmatism. Here, we present a high‐resolution, 3‐D resistivity model extending to 135 km depth, derived from broadband magnetotelluric data across the central JNO. The model reveals a predominantly resistive crust, disrupted by prominent conductors within the middle‐to‐lower crust beneath the Jiuling batholith. In the lithospheric mantle, a sharp electrical discontinuity coincides with the Early Neoproterozoic collisional zone (ca. 850–820 Ma), separating a resistive northwest from a conductive southeast. Integrated with geophysical and thermodynamic modeling, our findings suggest that present‐day partial melting beneath the Jiuling batholith is unlikely. Instead, the crustal conductors are interpreted as fossilized remnants of ancient tectono‐magmatic processes, likely representing trapped saline fluids or conductive mineral networks (e.g., graphite or sulfides). The upper mantle conductor is similarly interpreted as a fossilized subduction‐modified domain, potentially enriched in fluorine‐bearing phlogopite. Geodynamically, we propose that rollback and subsequent break‐off of the Paleo–South China Ocean slab induced substantial asthenospheric upwelling. This thermal perturbation drove extensive remelting of the thickened accretionary crust, generating the voluminous Jiuling granites. Our results delineate an integrated evolutionary pathway from oceanic subduction to mantle‐driven orogenesis, providing new insights into crustal maturation and Phanerozoic reactivation in ancient accretionary systems.

item.title: Poromechanical and Crack Evolution of Olivine‐Rich Rock During Serpentinization
item.dc:creator: Ummu‐kulthum Lawal
Kiseok Kim
item.description: Abstract Serpentinization alters the strength, fracture behavior, and rheology of ultramafic rocks, yet the coupled evolution of mineral reactions, cracking, and mechanical softening remains poorly constrained. We experimentally simulated serpentinization in dunite at 220°C and 15 MPa for 14 and 30 days and compared the reacted specimens with dry heat‐treated controls. Poromechanical tests and petrophysical measurements showed that serpentinization reduced the drained and unjacketed bulk moduli by up to 50% after 30 days. X‐ray diffraction (XRD) confirmed progressive olivine‐to‐serpentine replacement associated with solid‐volume expansion, while micro‐CT imaging captured reaction‐driven microcrack initiation and propagation. Crack‐density analysis showed that serpentinization generates microcracks but also partially seals larger features through secondary mineral growth, consistent with mercury intrusion porosimetry results. These results reveal a coupled process in which serpentinization simultaneously produces and infills fractures, reshaping the rock's mechanical framework. The findings clarify mechanisms governing seismic velocity reductions and hydromechanical weakening in serpentinizing mantle environments. Published: 2026-05-21T11:44:37+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.562 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ummu‐kulthum Lawal; Kiseok Kim

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl120883

Relevance: 0.562 (consider)

Abstract Serpentinization alters the strength, fracture behavior, and rheology of ultramafic rocks, yet the coupled evolution of mineral reactions, cracking, and mechanical softening remains poorly constrained. We experimentally simulated serpentinization in dunite at 220°C and 15 MPa for 14 and 30 days and compared the reacted specimens with dry heat‐treated controls. Poromechanical tests and petrophysical measurements showed that serpentinization reduced the drained and unjacketed bulk moduli by up to 50% after 30 days. X‐ray diffraction (XRD) confirmed progressive olivine‐to‐serpentine replacement associated with solid‐volume expansion, while micro‐CT imaging captured reaction‐driven microcrack initiation and propagation. Crack‐density analysis showed that serpentinization generates microcracks but also partially seals larger features through secondary mineral growth, consistent with mercury intrusion porosimetry results. These results reveal a coupled process in which serpentinization simultaneously produces and infills fractures, reshaping the rock's mechanical framework. The findings clarify mechanisms governing seismic velocity reductions and hydromechanical weakening in serpentinizing mantle environments.

item.title: Hierarchy of processes for lithium mobility and enrichment in anatectic crust
item.dc:creator: Omar Bartoli
Bruna B. Carvalho
Antonio Acosta-Vigil
Maurizio Petrelli
Tommaso Tacchetto
William D.A. Rickard
Markus Wälle
item.description: Rare-metal granites and pegmatites are commonly interpreted to form either by extreme fractionation of granitic magmas or by low-degree crustal melting. Despite renewed interest in the anatectic model, the mechanisms governing Li release into melts and whether crustal melting alone can concentrate Li to ore-grade levels remain debated. Here, we present the first comprehensive database of Li concentrations in anatectic melt inclusions, providing direct constraints on the Li budget of primary melts formed under typical mid- to lower-crustal pressure-temperature (P-T) conditions and compositions. The dataset shows that Li release into anatectic melts is optimized during early, fluid-absent equilibrium melting of biotite at 750−800 °C in cordierite-free rocks, reaching up to ∼600 μg/g Li. Although these concentrations exceed those of S-type granites, they overlap the range of barren pegmatites, indicating that extreme fractionation is required to produce hard-rock Li ores. Integration of natural constraints with petrological modeling suggests that direct generation of ore-forming Li enrichments (3000−20,000 μg/g Li) requires strongly pre-enriched sources (600−2000 μg/g Li, i.e., >20 times ordinary crustal abundances). Given the lack of compelling evidence that such sedimentary sources retain Li anomalies up to anatectic conditions, the formation of hard-rock Li ores of anatectic origin likely reflects the convergence in space and time of favorable melting mechanisms, P-T conditions, and tectonic controls enabling rapid melt extraction and extensive fractionation—a convergence that, although rare, is preferentially achieved during the waning stages of orogenic cycles. Published: 2026-05-22T21:25:46+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.562 (consider)

Authors: Omar Bartoli; Bruna B. Carvalho; Antonio Acosta-Vigil; Maurizio Petrelli; Tommaso Tacchetto; William D.A. Rickard; Markus Wälle

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54487.1

Relevance: 0.562 (consider)

Rare-metal granites and pegmatites are commonly interpreted to form either by extreme fractionation of granitic magmas or by low-degree crustal melting. Despite renewed interest in the anatectic model, the mechanisms governing Li release into melts and whether crustal melting alone can concentrate Li to ore-grade levels remain debated. Here, we present the first comprehensive database of Li concentrations in anatectic melt inclusions, providing direct constraints on the Li budget of primary melts formed under typical mid- to lower-crustal pressure-temperature (P-T) conditions and compositions. The dataset shows that Li release into anatectic melts is optimized during early, fluid-absent equilibrium melting of biotite at 750−800 °C in cordierite-free rocks, reaching up to ∼600 μg/g Li. Although these concentrations exceed those of S-type granites, they overlap the range of barren pegmatites, indicating that extreme fractionation is required to produce hard-rock Li ores. Integration of natural constraints with petrological modeling suggests that direct generation of ore-forming Li enrichments (3000−20,000 μg/g Li) requires strongly pre-enriched sources (600−2000 μg/g Li, i.e., >20 times ordinary crustal abundances). Given the lack of compelling evidence that such sedimentary sources retain Li anomalies up to anatectic conditions, the formation of hard-rock Li ores of anatectic origin likely reflects the convergence in space and time of favorable melting mechanisms, P-T conditions, and tectonic controls enabling rapid melt extraction and extensive fractionation—a convergence that, although rare, is preferentially achieved during the waning stages of orogenic cycles.

item.title: Biotite/melt trace-element, lithium, and F-OH partitioning in silicate magmas
item.dc:creator: Charles D Beard
Vincent J Hinsberg
John Stix
Madeleine C S Humphreys
Owen M Weller
Caroline R Soderman
Jean H Bédard
item.description: Abstract Biotite is a key hydrous silicate mineral in evolved magmatic systems, but its control on the behaviour of minor- and trace-elements, in particular Li, Nb, F and the REE is not well understood. Here, we quantify that control in sodic (per) alkaline H2O-saturated magmas with variable F-content through crystallisation experiments at 650–800°C and 200 MPa total pressure, at log fO2 ≈ FMQ +1. Biotite-glass pairs from tephriphonolite to phonolite fall deposits from Tenerife, Canary Islands, and a broad compilation from literature, complement our experimental data set. The new biotite-melt KdF/OH exchange coefficients are 2.9–47.0, typically 6.5–12.5, with minima for Al-rich, Mg-poor biotite. Nernst partition coefficients (D) silicate melt. D values for the large alkali metal ions Na–Cs define Onuma parabolae consistent with their incorporation on the biotite XIIA-site. Niobium partition coefficients are 0.1–1.2, and highest in Ti-rich biotite. The REE and actinides have D values less than 0.01 as their ionic radii fall between the size of the XIIA-site and VIM-sites of biotite. Our data, alongside a literature compilation, constrain empirical models that: (1) describe the exchange of F and OH between the silicate melt and the biotite W-site; (2) predict the partitioning of 1+ cations Li–Cs between silicate melt and the biotite A- and M-sites; (3) predict DNb values. The models use the major-element composition of biotite and silicate melt, pressure and temperature as input. Models are calibrated for use over a wide range of pressure, temperature and bulk composition (P-T-X) and can be used to interrogate biotite from natural systems to determine the composition of their source melt, or to forward model the trace-element evolution of mafic to evolved peralkaline syenite or peraluminous granite systems at upper-mantle to crustal pressures. Published: 2026-05-20T11:45:46+00:00 Venue: Journal of Petrology Score: 0.556 (consider)

Journal: Journal of Petrology

Abstract Biotite is a key hydrous silicate mineral in evolved magmatic systems, but its control on the behaviour of minor- and trace-elements, in particular Li, Nb, F and the REE is not well understood. Here, we quantify that control in sodic (per) alkaline H2O-saturated magmas with variable F-content through crystallisation experiments at 650–800°C and 200 MPa total pressure, at log fO2 ≈ FMQ +1. The new biotite-melt KdF/OH exchange coefficients are 2.9–47.0, typically 6.5–12.5, with minima for Al-rich, Mg-poor biotite. Nernst partition coefficients (D) silicate melt. D values for the large alkali metal ions Na–Cs define Onuma parabolae consistent with their incorporation on the biotite XIIA-site. Niobium partition coefficients are 0.1–1.2, and highest in Ti-rich biotite. The REE and actinides have D values less than 0.01 as their ionic radii fall between the size of the XIIA-site and VIM-sites of biotite. Our data, alongside a literature compilation, constrain empirical models that: (1) describe the exchange of F and OH between the silicate melt and the biotite W-site; (2) predict the partitioning of 1+ cations Li–Cs between silicate melt and the biotite A- and M-sites; (3) predict DNb values. The models use the major-element composition of biotite and silicate melt, pressure and temperature as input. Models are calibrated for use over a wide range of pressure, temperature and bulk composition (P-T-X) and can be used to interrogate biotite from natural systems to determine the composition of their source melt, or to forward model the trace-element evolution of mafic to evolved peralkaline syenite or peraluminous granite systems at upper-mantle to crustal pressures.

item.title: Linking the rheology of Himalayan leucogranites to formation of lithium pegmatites
item.dc:creator: Junyu Li
Kezhang Qin
Junxing Zhao
Xiaochi Liu
Wei Lin
Lei Jin
Liqun Zhu
Xinyu Zou
Mingjian Cao
Yuchao Liu
Changtong He
item.description: As one of the world’s largest extensional structures, the South Tibetan Detachment System (STDS) is spatially and temporally associated with Himalayan leucogranites and spodumene pegmatites. This study investigates variably deformed leucogranites near the Qiongjiagang lithium deposit in southern Tibet (Everest/Himalaya region) using field observations, mineral crystallographic preferred orientations, rheological parameters, and zircon geochronology. Key findings include the following: (1) Leucogranites within the STDS record systematically variable strain, with weakly deformed bodies preserving primary magmatic fabrics and hosting abundant spodumene pegmatites at higher structural levels. (2) Zircons within strongly deformed leucogranites exhibit significant fluid alteration. Weakly modified zircons yield ages of ca. 24 Ma, while hydrothermally altered zircons date to ca. 18 Ma. (3) Quantitative deformation analysis reveals that strongly deformed leucogranites record deformation temperatures of 450−590 °C, differential stresses of ∼36.5−75.2 MPa, and strain rates of ∼10−13 s−1 to 10−12 s−1, whereas weakly deformed leucogranites record higher temperatures (∼720 °C) and strain rates (∼10−10 s−1) under comparable stress conditions. (4) Comparative rheological analysis reveals that spodumene pegmatite emplacement requires specific mechanical conditions—a differential stress threshold of 26 MPa and strain rates of ∼10−10 s−1. Through a case study of the Qiongjiagang lithium deposit, this work elucidates the role of the STDS in leucogranite emplacement and evolution and provides quantitative constraints on the rheological conditions that facilitate the formation of spodumene pegmatites within the STDS. Published: 2026-05-19T20:05:21+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.555 (consider)

As one of the world’s largest extensional structures, the South Tibetan Detachment System (STDS) is spatially and temporally associated with Himalayan leucogranites and spodumene pegmatites. This study investigates variably deformed leucogranites near the Qiongjiagang lithium deposit in southern Tibet (Everest/Himalaya region) using field observations, mineral crystallographic preferred orientations, rheological parameters, and zircon geochronology. Key findings include the following: (1) Leucogranites within the STDS record systematically variable strain, with weakly deformed bodies preserving primary magmatic fabrics and hosting abundant spodumene pegmatites at higher structural levels. (2) Zircons within strongly deformed leucogranites exhibit significant fluid alteration. Weakly modified zircons yield ages of ca. 24 Ma, while hydrothermally altered zircons date to ca. 18 Ma. (3) Quantitative deformation analysis reveals that strongly deformed leucogranites record deformation temperatures of 450−590 °C, differential stresses of ∼36.5−75.2 MPa, and strain rates of ∼10−13 s−1 to 10−12 s−1, whereas weakly deformed leucogranites record higher temperatures (∼720 °C) and strain rates (∼10−10 s−1) under comparable stress conditions. (4) Comparative rheological analysis reveals that spodumene pegmatite emplacement requires specific mechanical conditions—a differential stress threshold of 26 MPa and strain rates of ∼10−10 s−1. Through a case study of the Qiongjiagang lithium deposit, this work elucidates the role of the STDS in leucogranite emplacement and evolution and provides quantitative constraints on the rheological conditions that facilitate the formation of spodumene pegmatites within the STDS.

item.title: Paleo–salt water dominates coastal aquifer salinization: A continental-scale study in China
item.dc:creator: Tianyuan Zheng
Bo Guo
Qinpeng Chang
Qiguo Sun
Xiaogang Chen
Xilai Zheng
Jichun Wu
Zhihong Zheng
Shaobo Gao
Chunmiao Zheng
Junguo Liu
Dong Wang
Tao Liu
Xiaolei Liu
Guangquan Chen
Xingyong Xu
Bo Zhang
Lin Zhang
Yajie Wu
Olaf Kolditz
Jian Luo
Alberto Guadagnini
item.description: Seawater intrusion poses a major threat to freshwater resources in coastal aquifers worldwide. In China alone, these types of aquifers provide a vital groundwater source for nearly 400 million people. Whereas modern seawater intrusion is often attributed to human activities and climate change, the role of paleo–salt water remains poorly quantified. This continental-scale investigation of seawater intrusion in China reveals that paleo–salt water, not modern seawater, dominates salinity patterns in large sedimentary basins. Analysis of over 2100 water samples shows a strong correlation between intrusion and tectonic subsidence zones, with paleo–salt water affecting 66% of the observed salinized areas. In northern coastal plains, paleo–salt water trapped in confined aquifers is characterized by salinity levels exceeding 144.6 grams per liter, whereas modern seawater intrusion (34%) occurs in hilly regions with thinner sediments. Isotopic signatures (δ 18 O) trace paleo–salt water intrusion to Quaternary sea level changes. These findings improve seawater intrusion source identification and inform global salinity management. Published: 2026-05-20T18:00:40+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.551 (consider)

item.title: Micritization mechanism in penecontemporaneous carbonate rocks: coupled C − Ca cycles and constraints from diagenetic environments
item.dc:creator: Yunfeng Zhang

Rui Wang
Guanghui Wu
Shuai Huang
item.description: While micritization in marine carbonate sediments is traditionally attributed to microbial endolithic boring, the mechanisms governing abiotic micritization driven by meteoric fluids remain poorly constrained. Furthermore, separating these early diagenetic signals from subsequent burial overprints in ancient geological records has proven persistently challenging. To resolve this ambiguity, we target a succession of shallow-marine carbonates from an isolated atoll in the southern South China Sea devoid of burial diagenetic alteration. By integrating high-resolution petrography, micro-domain trace element geochemistry, and non-traditional calcium state isotope ( δ 44/40 Ca) systematics, we reconstruct the trajectory of mineral recrystallization within the meteoric diagenetic environment. Our results demonstrate that the intensity and textural characteristics of micritization are strictly governed by hydrogeochemical gradients across the meteoric vadose-hyporheic transition. In the gravity-driven vadose zone, rapid fluid flux promotes the flushing of solutes, leading to transport-controlled congruent dissolution and the formation of fabric-selective moldic porosity. Conversely, in the hyporheic zone where fluid velocity decreases significantly, solute transport shifts from advection dominance to diffusion dominance. Here, mineral transformation proceeds via the interface-coupled dissolution-precipitation mechanism, generating intense micritization characterized by optical darkening and abundant microporosity. Coupled C-Ca isotope systematics further constrain these kinetic processes. Micritized components exhibit synchronous depletions in δ 13 C values and Sr/Ca ratios, fingerprints of an open system buffered by soil-derived meteoric fluids. Crucially, micritized components record a significant negative δ 44/40 Ca excursion of ∼ 0.4‰. Specifically, micrite-dominated fabrics exhibit δ 44/40 Ca values 0.57‰ lower than grain-supported counterparts. This reveals a kinetic isotope effect where light 40 Ca is preferentially incorporated during the rapid crystallization associated with abiotic micritization. The proposed coupled hydrodynamic-geochemical model establishes that meteoric-controlled micritization is a fundamental abiotic process during early diagenesis. Furthermore, we suggest δ 44/40 Ca as a robust geochemical proxy for distinguishing kinetically controlled rapid precipitation from thermodynamically controlled equilibrium recrystallization Published: 2026-05-21T16:13:48+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.550 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yunfeng Zhang; Menghao Zhu; Wen Yan; Haizhou Qu; Kexin Guo; Weihai Xu; Xiyang Zhang; Fei Tan; Yu Tang; Rui Wang; Guanghui Wu; Shuai Huang

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.05.018

Relevance: 0.550 (consider)

While micritization in marine carbonate sediments is traditionally attributed to microbial endolithic boring, the mechanisms governing abiotic micritization driven by meteoric fluids remain poorly constrained. Furthermore, separating these early diagenetic signals from subsequent burial overprints in ancient geological records has proven persistently challenging. To resolve this ambiguity, we target a succession of shallow-marine carbonates from an isolated atoll in the southern South China Sea devoid of burial diagenetic alteration. By integrating high-resolution petrography, micro-domain trace element geochemistry, and non-traditional calcium state isotope ( δ 44/40 Ca) systematics, we reconstruct the trajectory of mineral recrystallization within the meteoric diagenetic environment. Our results demonstrate that the intensity and textural characteristics of micritization are strictly governed by hydrogeochemical gradients across the meteoric vadose-hyporheic transition. In the gravity-driven vadose zone, rapid fluid flux promotes the flushing of solutes, leading to transport-controlled congruent dissolution and the formation of fabric-selective moldic porosity. Conversely, in the hyporheic zone where fluid velocity decreases significantly, solute transport shifts from advection dominance to diffusion dominance. Here, mineral transformation proceeds via the interface-coupled dissolution-precipitation mechanism, generating intense micritization characterized by optical darkening and abundant microporosity. Coupled C-Ca isotope systematics further constrain these kinetic processes. Micritized components exhibit synchronous depletions in δ 13 C values and Sr/Ca ratios, fingerprints of an open system buffered by soil-derived meteoric fluids. Crucially, micritized components record a significant negative δ 44/40 Ca excursion of ∼ 0.4‰. Specifically, micrite-dominated fabrics exhibit δ 44/40 Ca values 0.57‰ lower than grain-supported counterparts. This reveals a kinetic isotope effect where light 40 Ca is preferentially incorporated during the rapid crystallization associated with abiotic micritization. The proposed coupled hydrodynamic-geochemical model establishes that meteoric-controlled micritization is a fundamental abiotic process during early diagenesis. Furthermore, we suggest δ 44/40 Ca as a robust geochemical proxy for distinguishing kinetically controlled rapid precipitation from thermodynamically controlled equilibrium recrystallization

item.title: Kinematic inversion of the Xianshuihe-Xiaojiang fault system from Oligocene shortening to late Miocene left-lateral slip in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau
item.dc:creator: Guihong Zhang
Yuntao Tian
Junyi Li
Andrew V. Zuza
Bo Zhang
Zengjie Zhang
Peizhen Zhang
item.description: Several continental-scale strike-slip faults in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau developed during the Cenozoic eastward growth of the plateau. However, geodynamic mechanisms driving plateau growth and their relationship to intracontinental deformation remain poorly resolved, primarily because the brittle deformation histories of major fault systems—such as the >1000-km-long Xianshuihe-Xiaojiang (XSH-XJ) fault system—are inadequately constrained and difficult to reconstruct. To address this gap, we performed thermochronological analyses of samples from the highest mountains (i.e., Jiaozi Shan and Dahaicao Shan) flanking the XJ segment of the fault system, including apatite (U-Th)/He, apatite fission track, and zircon (U-Th)/He dating, complemented by thermal history modeling. Results reveal differential exhumation and cooling across the XJ fault between ca. 26 Ma and 22 Ma, with greater cooling in the Jiaozi Shan (30−50 °C) than the Dahaicao Shan (<5 °C), implying an exhumation offset of 1−2 km. Since ca. 13 Ma, rapid cooling (50−70 °C) has occurred synchronously on both sides of the XJ fault, corresponding to ∼2 km of accelerated exhumation. Together with structural analyses, it is interpreted that the two phases of exhumation and cooling were kinematically linked to a transition of deformation from late Oligocene east-vergent thrusting to late Miocene left-slip faulting. Integrating structural and geophysical data along the XSH-XJ fault system, we propose that the Oligocene shortening caused crustal thickening and in situ anatexis. These processes produced thermally and mechanically weakened lower crust, facilitating the late Miocene initiation of left-lateral slip. Our work highlights a dynamic linkage between the two phases of deformation, providing new insights into how kinematic inversions of major faults operate in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau. Published: 2026-05-20T20:20:38+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.549 (consider)

Authors: Guihong Zhang; Yuntao Tian; Junyi Li; Andrew V. Zuza; Bo Zhang; Zengjie Zhang; Peizhen Zhang

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38765.1

Relevance: 0.549 (consider)

Several continental-scale strike-slip faults in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau developed during the Cenozoic eastward growth of the plateau. However, geodynamic mechanisms driving plateau growth and their relationship to intracontinental deformation remain poorly resolved, primarily because the brittle deformation histories of major fault systems—such as the >1000-km-long Xianshuihe-Xiaojiang (XSH-XJ) fault system—are inadequately constrained and difficult to reconstruct. To address this gap, we performed thermochronological analyses of samples from the highest mountains (i.e., Jiaozi Shan and Dahaicao Shan) flanking the XJ segment of the fault system, including apatite (U-Th)/He, apatite fission track, and zircon (U-Th)/He dating, complemented by thermal history modeling. Results reveal differential exhumation and cooling across the XJ fault between ca. 26 Ma and 22 Ma, with greater cooling in the Jiaozi Shan (30−50 °C) than the Dahaicao Shan (<5 °C), implying an exhumation offset of 1−2 km. Since ca. 13 Ma, rapid cooling (50−70 °C) has occurred synchronously on both sides of the XJ fault, corresponding to ∼2 km of accelerated exhumation. Together with structural analyses, it is interpreted that the two phases of exhumation and cooling were kinematically linked to a transition of deformation from late Oligocene east-vergent thrusting to late Miocene left-slip faulting. Integrating structural and geophysical data along the XSH-XJ fault system, we propose that the Oligocene shortening caused crustal thickening and in situ anatexis. These processes produced thermally and mechanically weakened lower crust, facilitating the late Miocene initiation of left-lateral slip. Our work highlights a dynamic linkage between the two phases of deformation, providing new insights into how kinematic inversions of major faults operate in the southeastern Tibetan Plateau.

item.title: Scandium enrichment and release in pyroxene driven by melt and fluid metasomatism: Insights from East Gondwana, Antarctica
item.dc:creator: Shan-Shan Li
Bing Li
You-Hong Sun
Jun Deng
German Leitchenkov
Kristoffer Szilas
David Hernández-Uribe
Pavel Talalay
Nan Zhang
Xiao-Peng Fan
Ya-Zhou Li
Da Gong
Yi-Zi Zou
Jin-Hua Li
Kun-Feng Qiu
item.description: Abstract Mafic-ultramafic intrusions represent the primary source of global scandium (Sc) resources worldwide. These intrusions typically originate from fertile mantle sources in arc systems. While fluid- and melt-driven metasomatism are widely recognized as crucial mechanisms for Sc enrichment in the mantle, key aspects, including the precise composition of these metasomatic agents and their operating conditions remain poorly constrained. To address these knowledge gaps, we conducted a comprehensive investigation of Sc enrichment and release using clinopyroxene and orthopyroxene chemistry from charnockites and mafic granulites in East Antarctica’s Prydz Bay Belt. We reveal a multi-stage Sc evolution pathway within the accretionary belt that is initiated by dehydration of carbonate rocks, generating reduced carbonatite melts which trigger mantle metasomatism and significantly enrich Sc in the pyroxene. This enrichment is followed by post-peak decompression melting at 800-1000 °C and 6.5-12.8 kbar, which triggers metasomatism by an oxidized silicate melt, facilitating Sc release from both orthopyroxene and clinopyroxene. The subsequent arc accretion stage involves interaction with an external, oxidized, and likely phosphorus-rich aqueous fluid, leading to Sc release. The findings of Sc-rich pyroxene in mafic granulite and charnockite, together with the presence of CO₂-rich fluid inclusions in East Gondwana (Antarctica), collectively indicate that scandium enrichment may be a regional event. This study provides further implications for the exploration of Sc deposits in metamorphic rocks. Published: 2026-05-20T20:44:33+00:00 Venue: American Mineralogist Score: 0.548 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shan-Shan Li; Bing Li; You-Hong Sun; Jun Deng; German Leitchenkov; Kristoffer Szilas; David Hernández-Uribe; Pavel Talalay; Nan Zhang; Xiao-Peng Fan; Ya-Zhou Li; Da Gong; Yi-Zi Zou; Jin-Hua Li; Kun-Feng Qiu

Journal: American Mineralogist

DOI: 10.2138/am-2025-10035

Relevance: 0.548 (ignore)

Abstract Mafic-ultramafic intrusions represent the primary source of global scandium (Sc) resources worldwide. These intrusions typically originate from fertile mantle sources in arc systems. While fluid- and melt-driven metasomatism are widely recognized as crucial mechanisms for Sc enrichment in the mantle, key aspects, including the precise composition of these metasomatic agents and their operating conditions remain poorly constrained. To address these knowledge gaps, we conducted a comprehensive investigation of Sc enrichment and release using clinopyroxene and orthopyroxene chemistry from charnockites and mafic granulites in East Antarctica’s Prydz Bay Belt. We reveal a multi-stage Sc evolution pathway within the accretionary belt that is initiated by dehydration of carbonate rocks, generating reduced carbonatite melts which trigger mantle metasomatism and significantly enrich Sc in the pyroxene. This enrichment is followed by post-peak decompression melting at 800-1000 °C and 6.5-12.8 kbar, which triggers metasomatism by an oxidized silicate melt, facilitating Sc release from both orthopyroxene and clinopyroxene. The subsequent arc accretion stage involves interaction with an external, oxidized, and likely phosphorus-rich aqueous fluid, leading to Sc release. The findings of Sc-rich pyroxene in mafic granulite and charnockite, together with the presence of CO₂-rich fluid inclusions in East Gondwana (Antarctica), collectively indicate that scandium enrichment may be a regional event. This study provides further implications for the exploration of Sc deposits in metamorphic rocks.

item.title: Isotopic evidence for a long-lived Hadean basaltic–andesitic precursor to the Acasta Gneiss Complex, Canada
item.dc:creator: Alessandro Maltese
Guillaume Caro
Klaus Mezger
Jasper Berndt
Peter Sprung
Dieter Garbe-Schönberg
Wouter Bleeker
Erik E. Scherer
item.description: Understanding the genesis and composition of Earth’s earliest crust is critical for reconstructing the planet’s early differentiation and geodynamic evolution. However, direct evidence from the Hadean (>4.0 Ga) remains elusive owing to the near-complete absence of preserved rocks from that eon. Paleoarchean layered gneisses from the Acasta Gneiss Complex (AGC, Northwest Territories, Canada) preserve isotope signatures inherited from a Hadean crustal precursor. To investigate the petrogenesis of this precursor, whole-rock 146,147 Sm‒ 142,143 Nd data were obtained from a suite of samples cut parallel to the compositional layering of the gneiss to obtain chemically and mineralogically diverse rock slices. Additional analyses, including whole-rock major and trace element compositions, 176 Lu‒ 176 Hf isotope systematics, and apatite ( 147 Sm‒ 143 Nd and 176 Lu‒ 176 Hf) and zircon (U‒Pb) geochronology, were conducted to evaluate the preservation of primary isotope systematics and reconstruct the evolutionary history of the AGC. Published: 2026-05-19T23:18:01+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.548 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Alessandro Maltese; Guillaume Caro; Klaus Mezger; Jasper Berndt; Peter Sprung; Dieter Garbe-Schönberg; Wouter Bleeker; Erik E. Scherer

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.05.017

Relevance: 0.548 (ignore)

Understanding the genesis and composition of Earth’s earliest crust is critical for reconstructing the planet’s early differentiation and geodynamic evolution. However, direct evidence from the Hadean (>4.0 Ga) remains elusive owing to the near-complete absence of preserved rocks from that eon. Paleoarchean layered gneisses from the Acasta Gneiss Complex (AGC, Northwest Territories, Canada) preserve isotope signatures inherited from a Hadean crustal precursor. To investigate the petrogenesis of this precursor, whole-rock 146,147 Sm‒ 142,143 Nd data were obtained from a suite of samples cut parallel to the compositional layering of the gneiss to obtain chemically and mineralogically diverse rock slices. Additional analyses, including whole-rock major and trace element compositions, 176 Lu‒ 176 Hf isotope systematics, and apatite ( 147 Sm‒ 143 Nd and 176 Lu‒ 176 Hf) and zircon (U‒Pb) geochronology, were conducted to evaluate the preservation of primary isotope systematics and reconstruct the evolutionary history of the AGC.

item.title: Seismic radial anisotropy beneath Mount Etna (Sicily, Italy) generated by deep mantle upwelling
item.dc:creator: Yuanyuan Hua
Yang Yu
Dapeng Zhao
Yi-Gang Xu
Xiao-Long Huang
Hong-Yan Li
Fan Yang
Siwen Zhang
Yue Yuan
item.description: The central Mediterranean provides a key setting to investigate links between mantle upwelling, subduction, and arc volcanism. In southern Italy, rapid slab retreat and segmentation of the Calabrian subduction zone favor mantle flow, yet direct seismic evidence for mantle upwelling remains scarce. Here we present 3-D radial anisotropic tomography of the Calabrian subduction zone, derived from joint inversion of local and teleseismic traveltime data. Our results show a pronounced low-velocity (low-V) anomaly beneath Mount Etna (Sicily, Italy), located at the Ionian slab edge and connected to a broader low-V zone beneath the northeastern African margin. The anomaly exhibits negative radial anisotropy, consistent with vertical mantle flow, but its relatively weak magnitude suggests a waning upwelling flux. This feature extends at least below the mantle transition zone, favoring a contribution from the lower mantle. Our findings provide new seismic constraints on the mantle source of Etna and its geodynamic links to subduction processes. Published: 2026-05-20T20:20:37+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.548 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuanyuan Hua; Yang Yu; Dapeng Zhao; Yi-Gang Xu; Xiao-Long Huang; Hong-Yan Li; Fan Yang; Siwen Zhang; Yue Yuan

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38999.1

Relevance: 0.548 (ignore)

The central Mediterranean provides a key setting to investigate links between mantle upwelling, subduction, and arc volcanism. In southern Italy, rapid slab retreat and segmentation of the Calabrian subduction zone favor mantle flow, yet direct seismic evidence for mantle upwelling remains scarce. Here we present 3-D radial anisotropic tomography of the Calabrian subduction zone, derived from joint inversion of local and teleseismic traveltime data. Our results show a pronounced low-velocity (low-V) anomaly beneath Mount Etna (Sicily, Italy), located at the Ionian slab edge and connected to a broader low-V zone beneath the northeastern African margin. The anomaly exhibits negative radial anisotropy, consistent with vertical mantle flow, but its relatively weak magnitude suggests a waning upwelling flux. This feature extends at least below the mantle transition zone, favoring a contribution from the lower mantle. Our findings provide new seismic constraints on the mantle source of Etna and its geodynamic links to subduction processes.
